walkthrough.title: Find the Right Cards for Your Player Count
walkthrough.text: Let's get Love Letter set up. First, check how many players you have. If you have two to four players, use the base deck of 16 cards. If you have five or six players, add in the five extra cards marked with a small star icon. These extra cards include another Guard, Priest, Baron, Handmaid, and Prince. The total deck will be 21 cards for the larger game.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Remove Cards
walkthrough.text: Now shuffle the deck thoroughly and place it face down in the center of the table. Take the top card and set it aside face down without looking at it. This unknown card adds uncertainty to every round because nobody knows which card is missing. If you are playing with only two players, also remove three additional cards face up so both players can see them. This gives you extra information to work with.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Deal one card face down to each player. Go ahead and look at your card, but keep it secret from everyone else. This single card is your entire hand. You will always have just one or two cards at any given moment. Place the remaining deck in the center where everyone can reach it.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Tokens and Pick First Player
walkthrough.text: Place the tokens of affection in a shared pile in the center. These are the red cubes used to track round wins. The number of tokens needed to win the game depends on player count: six tokens for two players, five for three, four for four, three for five, and three for six. Pick a starting player by any method you like. Play will go clockwise from there.

summary.title: Find the Right Cards for Your Player Count
bullets: 2-4 players: use base 16-card deck
5-6 players: add 5 starred cards for 21-card deck
Extra cards: 1 Guard, 1 Priest, 1 Baron, 1 Handmaid, 1 Prince

summary.title: Shuffle and Remove Cards
bullets: Shuffle deck, place face-down in center
Remove top card face-down (unknown to all)
2-player only: also remove 3 cards face-up

summary.title: Deal Starting Hands
bullets: Deal 1 card face-down to each player
Look at your card — keep it secret
Remaining cards form the draw pile

summary.title: Set Out Tokens and Pick First Player
bullets: Place token of affection cubes in center
Win target: 2p=6, 3p=5, 4p=4, 5p=3, 6p=3
Pick starting player, play clockwise

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Love Letter is a fast deduction and bluffing game. Each round, players take turns drawing a card and playing a card, using card effects to try to eliminate other players or deduce what they are holding. The last player standing wins the round, or if the deck runs out, the player holding the highest card wins.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn in Three Seconds
walkthrough.text: On your turn, draw one card from the draw pile so you now have two cards in hand. Then choose one of your two cards and play it face up in front of you. Resolve the played card's effect immediately. That is your entire turn. Simple as that.

walkthrough.title: The Low Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through each card from lowest to highest. The Spy is worth zero and has no immediate effect, but if you are the only player who played or discarded a Spy during the round and you are still alive at the end, you gain a bonus token. The Guard is worth one and lets you name any non-Guard card and choose another player. If that player has the named card, they are eliminated from the round. The Priest is worth two and lets you secretly look at another player's hand.

walkthrough.title: The Middle Cards
walkthrough.text: The Baron is worth three and forces you to compare your remaining hand card with another player's card. Whoever has the lower value is eliminated. If you tie, nothing happens. The Handmaid is worth four and protects you from being targeted by any card effect until the start of your next turn. The Prince is worth five and forces any one player, including yourself, to discard their hand and draw a new card from the deck.

walkthrough.title: The High Cards
walkthrough.text: The Chancellor is worth six and is new to the 2019 edition. When you play it, draw two cards from the deck, look at all three cards in your hand, keep one, and place the other two face down on the bottom of the deck. The King is worth seven and lets you trade your hand card with another player's hand card. The Countess is worth eight and has no effect when played, but you must play her if your other card is a King or a Prince.

walkthrough.title: The Princess and Elimination
walkthrough.text: The Princess is worth nine, the highest card in the game. If you play or discard the Princess for any reason, you are immediately eliminated from the round. This means holding the Princess is powerful for comparisons and endgame, but dangerous if someone forces you to discard with a Prince. When you are eliminated, lay your hand card face up so everyone can see it.

walkthrough.title: Handmaid Protection and Targeting
walkthrough.text: When a Handmaid is in play, the protected player cannot be chosen as a target for any card effect. However, if all other players are protected by Handmaids, you may target a protected player because you have no other legal choice. Remember, Handmaid protection wears off at the start of your next turn, not at the end.

walkthrough.title: Winning Rounds and Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: A round ends when the draw pile runs out or only one player remains. If multiple players are still alive when the deck empties, everyone reveals their hand card and the highest value wins. Tied players each receive a token. The winner of each round takes one token of affection. The first player to collect the required number of tokens wins the entire game.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: Fast deduction/bluffing game — draw 1, play 1, resolve effect
Eliminate opponents or hold highest card when deck empties
Win rounds → earn tokens → first to target wins the game

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Round
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first round of Love Letter! We will walk through a practice round with four players. Even if you have a different number at your table, follow along to see how the cards interact. The deck has been shuffled, one card removed face down, and everyone has drawn their starting card. Let's play.

walkthrough.title: Player One Draws and Plays
walkthrough.text: Player one draws a card and now holds a Guard and a Priest. This is a common opening hand. Playing the Guard right away is tempting, but with no information yet, any guess is pure luck. Instead, Player one plays the Priest and looks at Player two's hand. Now Player one knows exactly what Player two is holding. That information is gold for a future Guard guess.

walkthrough.title: Why Information Comes First
walkthrough.text: This is one of the most important lessons in Love Letter. A Guard guess without information is roughly a one-in-seven chance of being right. But if you use a Priest first, your next Guard guess becomes a guaranteed elimination. Always gather information before spending your Guards whenever possible.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Draws and Plays
walkthrough.text: Player two draws a card and now holds a Handmaid and a Prince. Player two knows that Player one just saw their hand, which means Player one might target them with a Guard next turn. Playing the Handmaid is the smart move here because it blocks all targeting until Player two's next turn. Player two plays the Handmaid and is now safe.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Blind Guard Guess
walkthrough.text: Player three draws and gets a Guard and a Baron. Player three gets excited and immediately plays the Guard, targeting Player four and guessing Princess. Princess seems like a dramatic guess, but there is only one Princess in the entire deck. The guess misses. Player three just wasted a powerful card on a long-shot gamble.

walkthrough.title: Why Random Guessing Fails
walkthrough.text: Here is the math. With a 16-card deck and one card removed, a random Guard guess has terrible odds. Guessing Princess means you need one specific card out of many possibilities. Instead, Player three should have held the Guard and played the Baron. If their Baron comparison won, great. If not, at least they did not waste their Guard on a blind guess.

walkthrough.title: Player Four Takes a Turn
walkthrough.text: Player four draws and now holds a Chancellor and a Spy. The Chancellor is the stronger play here. Player four plays the Chancellor, draws two more cards from the deck, looks at all three, picks the best one, and places the other two on the bottom of the deck. The Chancellor gives incredible card selection and is almost always worth playing when you draw it.

walkthrough.title: Player One's Informed Guard
walkthrough.text: Player one draws and gets a Guard. Remember, Player one saw Player two's hand earlier with the Priest. If Player two still holds that same card and is not protected by a Handmaid, Player one can now use the Guard with a guaranteed correct guess. Player one targets Player two, names the card they saw, and Player two is eliminated. That is the power of combining Priest with Guard.

walkthrough.title: The Countess Trap
walkthrough.text: A few turns later, Player three draws the Countess while already holding a Prince. This triggers the forced-play rule. If you hold the Countess along with a King or a Prince, you must play the Countess. Player three has no choice and plays the Countess. But here is what the other players see: they know Player three must have a King or Prince. Watch out for that tell.

walkthrough.title: The Countess Bluff
walkthrough.text: Here is an advanced move to remember. Sometimes you can play the Countess even when you do not have a King or Prince. Your opponents will assume you were forced to play it, which means they think you are holding a seven or a five. This misdirection can protect your real card for the rest of the round. The Countess bluff is one of Love Letter's best tricks.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Forgetting Card Counting
walkthrough.text: Player four targets Player one with a Guard and guesses Baron. But both Barons have already been played face up on the table in previous turns. It is impossible for Player one to hold a Baron. Player four just wasted a Guard on a guess that could never succeed. Always check the discard pile before guessing.

walkthrough.title: The Round Ends
walkthrough.text: The deck runs out with two players remaining. Both reveal their hand cards. Player four holds a five and Player one holds a seven. Player one wins the round with the higher card and takes a token of affection. Shuffle all cards together, remove one face down, deal one to each player, and start a new round. First to four tokens wins the game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Play
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your first real game, here are three things to remember.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics like when to use each card and how to read your opponents.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every card and key rules.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Play
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick guide to every card
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Track Every Played Card
walkthrough.text: The single most important habit in Love Letter is watching the discard pile. Every card played goes face up, so by mid-round you can narrow down what is still in play. If both Guards have been played, nobody can Guard-guess you. If the Princess has appeared, the highest remaining card is the Countess. The more cards you track, the better your guesses and the safer your decisions become.

walkthrough.title: Save Guards for Informed Guesses
walkthrough.text: Guards are your most powerful elimination tool, but only when you have information. A blind guess is roughly one in seven at the start of a round. After a Priest peek, a Baron comparison, or watching several cards get played, those odds jump dramatically. Whenever possible, hold your Guard until you have strong evidence of what someone is holding. Playing a Guard on turn one with no information is almost always a waste.

walkthrough.title: Use the Handmaid Strategically
walkthrough.text: The Handmaid is your shield. Play it when you are holding a high-value card you want to protect, like the Princess or Countess. It is also smart to play the Handmaid when you suspect someone has a Guard lined up against you. One round of protection can be the difference between surviving to the endgame comparison and getting eliminated early.

walkthrough.title: Be Careful with the Princess
walkthrough.text: Holding the Princess means you will win any Baron comparison and any end-of-round showdown. But it also makes you incredibly vulnerable to the Prince, which forces a discard and instantly eliminates you. If you hold the Princess, play defensively. Use Handmaids for protection and avoid drawing attention to yourself. Sometimes it is actually safer to ditch the Princess early with a Prince on yourself rather than risk someone else forcing you to discard her.

walkthrough.title: Read the Countess Carefully
walkthrough.text: When someone plays the Countess, pay attention. They might have been forced to play it because they also held a King or Prince. Or they might be bluffing, playing the Countess voluntarily to make you think they hold a high card. Watch the context. If they played the Countess immediately after drawing, it was probably forced. If they hesitated or if the timing seems off, consider that it might be a bluff.

walkthrough.title: The Prince Is More Versatile Than You Think
walkthrough.text: New players often overlook using the Prince on themselves. But self-targeting is powerful when you are stuck with a low card late in the round. Discard your weak hand and draw something fresh from the deck. You might pick up the Princess or Countess for the endgame showdown. The Prince can also be used offensively to force an opponent to discard a high card, or to eliminate someone holding the Princess.

walkthrough.title: Pay Attention to Player Behavior
walkthrough.text: Love Letter is a social game, and people give away information through their actions. A player who suddenly plays a Handmaid probably just drew something valuable. A player who hesitates before playing might be weighing a difficult decision between two high cards. A player who confidently plays a Baron likely has a strong hand. Watch for these patterns because they are just as useful as card tracking.

walkthrough.title: Target the Leader
walkthrough.text: Always keep an eye on the token count. The player closest to winning should be your primary target whenever you have a choice of who to attack. There is no point eliminating the player with zero tokens when someone else needs just one more to win the whole game. Use your Guards, Barons, and Princes on the biggest threat first.

bullets: All played cards go face-up — watch the discard pile

If both copies of a card are gone, nobody can hold it

Sometimes safer to self-Prince early than risk forced discard

Offensive: force opponent to discard high card

One of the most flexible cards in the game

walkthrough.title: Card Counting Mastery
walkthrough.text: Advanced Love Letter play revolves around card counting. In a four-player game with 16 cards, one removed and four dealt means 11 cards in the draw pile. As each card is played face up, you can track exactly what remains. With two Guards, two Priests, two Barons, two Handmaids, two Princes, one Chancellor, one King, one Countess, and one Princess, even a few eliminations narrow the possibilities dramatically. By mid-round, you should know exactly which two or three cards could still be in opponents' hands.

walkthrough.title: The Priest-Guard Pipeline
walkthrough.text: The strongest two-turn sequence in Love Letter is Priest followed by Guard. Use the Priest to peek at a target's hand, then on your next turn play the Guard to name exactly what you saw. This works best early in the round before your target has a chance to play or swap the card you peeked at. If you draw both a Priest and a Guard at the same time, always play the Priest first. The guaranteed elimination on your next turn is worth far more than a blind guess now.

walkthrough.title: Baron Timing
walkthrough.text: The Baron is a high-risk, high-reward card. Use it when you are confident your remaining card is higher than your target's. The ideal Baron play happens when you hold a seven, eight, or nine and your opponent played low cards earlier, suggesting they are still holding something weak. Never Baron when you are holding a two or three unless you are desperate. A lost Baron comparison means immediate elimination with no second chance.

walkthrough.title: The Countess Bluff Deep Dive
walkthrough.text: Playing the Countess voluntarily is one of the most advanced plays in the game. When you play the Countess without holding a King or Prince, every opponent assumes you were forced and now believes you hold a five or seven. This misdirection has two benefits. First, opponents waste Guards guessing Prince or King. Second, they may avoid targeting you with a Baron because they think your card is high. The best time to bluff is when your actual card is middling, like a three or four, because the bluff buys you safety you would not otherwise have.

walkthrough.title: Chancellor Card Selection
walkthrough.text: The Chancellor gives you a peek at three cards and lets you keep the best one. When choosing, think about what threats remain. If Guards are still in play, keeping a Handmaid might save you. If few cards remain in the deck, keep the highest card for the endgame comparison. Also consider what you put on the bottom of the deck. Those two cards are out of play for the rest of the round, which narrows the remaining card pool. Use that knowledge for future Guard guesses.

walkthrough.title: King Swap Tactics
walkthrough.text: The King forces a hand swap, and the best time to play it is when you are holding a low card and suspect your opponent has something strong. A King swap when you hold a one or two and your opponent holds a seven or eight is a massive swing. But be careful because your opponent now knows exactly what you have. They know because you gave it to them. Play the King early before they get a chance to use the information against you on their next turn.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Positioning
walkthrough.text: When the deck is running low, shift your strategy entirely. Stop trying to eliminate opponents and start preparing for the comparison. Hold the highest card you can. If you are stuck with a low card, use a Prince on yourself to draw fresh. If you are holding high, play a Handmaid to ride out the final turns safely. Count the remaining cards in the deck because when it hits zero, the round is over instantly and whoever has the highest card wins.

walkthrough.title: Metagame and Multi-Round Strategy
walkthrough.text: Love Letter is played across multiple rounds, and your reputation carries between them. If you are known for Countess bluffs, opponents will stop falling for it. If you always Priest before Guard, opponents will Handmaid as soon as you peek at them. Mix up your play patterns across rounds. Also, manage your threat level. If you have three tokens and need four to win, every opponent will gang up on you. Sometimes it is better to play conservatively and avoid attention when you are close to winning rather than playing aggressively and becoming everyone's target.

summary.title: Card Counting Mastery
bullets: 16-card deck (4p): 1 removed + 4 dealt = 11 in draw pile
Track every played card → narrow down what opponents could hold
By mid-round, only 2-3 cards are unknown

summary.title: The Priest-Guard Pipeline
bullets: Priest peek → Guard guess next turn = guaranteed elimination
Play Priest first, Guard second — never reverse
Works best early before target plays or swaps the peeked card

summary.title: Baron Timing
bullets: Baron with 7/8/9 in hand → strong play
Baron with 2/3 → very risky, avoid unless desperate
Read opponent's played cards to estimate their hand value
Lost Baron = instant elimination, no recovery

summary.title: The Countess Bluff Deep Dive
bullets: Play Countess voluntarily → opponents assume forced (think you hold 5/7)
They waste Guards guessing Prince/King
Best when holding a 3 or 4 — bluff buys undeserved safety
Don't overuse — opponents catch on across rounds

summary.title: Chancellor Card Selection

Guards still in play? Keep Handmaid for safety
Low deck? Keep highest card for endgame comparison
Cards placed on deck bottom = out of play (use for deduction)

summary.title: King Swap Tactics
bullets: Swap low card for opponent's high card → massive swing
Warning: opponent now knows your exact card (you gave it to them)
Play King early so they can't exploit the info
Best when holding 1-2 and opponent likely has 7+

summary.title: Endgame Positioning
bullets: Deck running low → shift from elimination to comparison strategy
Hold highest card possible; self-Prince if stuck with low card
Handmaid to ride out final turns safely with a high card
Count remaining deck — 0 cards = instant round end

summary.title: Metagame and Multi-Round Strategy
bullets: Reputation carries between rounds — vary your patterns
If known for Countess bluffs, opponents stop falling for it
Close to winning? Play conservatively to avoid becoming everyone's target
Mix up Priest-Guard timing so opponents can't predict you

entries.definition: No immediate effect when played. At the end of the round, if you are the only player who played or discarded a Spy and you are still alive, you gain one bonus token of affection. Two copies in the deck.

Name any non-Guard card and choose another player. If that player holds the named card, they are eliminated from the round. The most common card in the deck with two copies (three in the five to six player deck).

Choose another player and secretly look at their hand card. Do not reveal it to other players. Two copies in the deck (three in the five to six player deck). Best paired with a Guard on your next turn.
Choose another player and secretly compare hand cards. The player with the lower value is eliminated. If tied, nothing happens. Two copies in the deck (three in the five to six player deck).

You cannot be targeted by any card effect until the start of your next turn. If all other players are protected, a player may target a protected player. Two copies in the deck (three in the five to six player deck).

Choose any player, including yourself. That player discards their hand card face up and draws a new card. If the discarded card is the Princess, that player is eliminated. Two copies in the deck (three in the five to six player deck).

New in the 2019 edition. Draw two cards from the deck, look at all three cards in your hand, keep one, and place the other two face down on the bottom of the deck in any order. Two copies in the deck. If fewer than two cards remain in the deck, draw as many as available.
Choose another player and trade hand cards with them. You give them your remaining card and take theirs. One copy in the deck. Cannot target a player protected by a Handmaid.

No effect when played. However, if your other card is a King (7) or Prince (5), you must play the Countess. You may also play the Countess voluntarily even without a King or Prince, which can serve as a bluff. One copy in the deck.
The highest value card in the game. If you play or discard the Princess for any reason, including being forced by a Prince, you are immediately eliminated from the round. One copy in the deck.

If you hold the Countess (8) along with a King (7) or Prince (5), you must play the Countess. This is mandatory and cannot be avoided. Other players can deduce you hold a King or Prince when this happens.

A player who has played a Handmaid cannot be chosen as the target of any card effect. Protection lasts until the start of that player's next turn. If all other players are protected, the active player may target a protected player.

When you are eliminated from a round, place your hand card face up in front of you. You take no more turns this round. Elimination happens from losing a Baron comparison, being correctly Guard-guessed, or playing/discarding the Princess.

Red cubes earned by winning rounds. The first player to reach the target number wins the game. Target: two players need six, three players need five, four players need four, five or six players need three.
If exactly one player played or discarded a Spy during the round and that player is still alive at round end, they gain one bonus token of affection in addition to any token from winning the round.
When the draw pile runs out, all surviving players reveal their hand cards. The player with the highest value card wins the round. If tied, all tied players receive a token of affection.

At the start of each round, one card is removed face down from the game. In two-player games, three additional cards are removed face up. The face-down card is never revealed and adds uncertainty to deduction.
For games with five or six players, add five extra cards marked with a star to the deck: one additional Guard, Priest, Baron, Handmaid, and Prince. This brings the deck from 16 to 21 cards.
